summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques the Value-Added Model (VAM) for teacher assessment after attending a lecture by Boston College professor Henry Braun. She draws a parallel between Wall Street's 'culling' mentality and the education reform movement's obsession with individual teacher accountability. The post argues that focusing on assessing and blaming individual teachers ignores systemic issues like unequal funding, curriculum problems, and school culture. O'Neil questions whether individual responsibility can solve what is fundamentally a collective, societal problem, connecting this to broader critiques of how American culture places systemic burdens on individuals.
key_points: The VAM approach to teacher assessment is an inconsistent 'black box' that may not actually improve education
There's a fundamental split between reformers who want to improve educational systems versus those who want to assess individual teachers
The 'assess the teachers' crowd ignores systemic factors: school culture, curriculum quality, unequal funding
Wall Street's 'cull the bottom 10%' mentality is being inappropriately applied to education
Placing systemic problems on individuals (like Oprah's self-help advice) ignores context and structural barriers
money_quotes: The same mindset which served the country so poorly is now somehow being held up as a success story, and applied to other fields like public education.
Why do we think individual responsibility – i.e. finger pointing at individual teachers – is going to improve the overall system?
Fuck the school culture, fuck communicating with other teachers in the school. Fuck differences in curriculum or having old books or not having enough books due to unequal funding.
The problem with Oprah's reasoning is that it ignores real systemic problems and issues that radically affect certain parts of the population and make it much harder to take her advice.
It's context free in a world where context is more and more meaningful.
Whose problem is the achievement gap? Is it owned in tiny pieces by every teacher who dares to enter the profession?
key_insight: Reducing systemic educational failures to individual teacher accountability is the same flawed thinking that let Wall Street escape responsibility for the financial crisis.
